videoTitle: GitHub vs GitLab: Which DevOps Platform is Best for Your Project?
videoDescription:
The ultimate fight for the modern developer workflow comes down to two giants: GitHub and GitLab. They both start with Git, but their fundamental philosophies on how you build, test, and deploy software couldn't be more different. Are you better off with GitHub's flexible "Best-of-Breed" Marketplace approach, or is GitLab's unified, "All-in-One" DevSecOps platform the right choice for your enterprise?

In this comprehensive comparison, I break down the core features, CI/CD platforms (GitHub Actions vs. GitLab CI/CD), security models, and hosting flexibility to help you make the definitive decision for your team, whether you're working on open-source, a complex enterprise pipeline, or a heavily regulated project.

Watch the full video to find out which platform is the absolute best fit for your specific needs!

What do you think? Which platform are you using for your current project and why? Let me know in the comments below!

If you enjoy the video,  like it, and subscribe to my channel!
See you in the next video!

videoTitle: Authentic DevOps with AI Foundry + GitHub, on Sip & Sync with Azure
videoDescription:
Join us for another exciting episode of Sip and Sync with Azure! ☁️🎤 Host Katie Novotny welcomes Krystal Folkes, Software Global Black Belt at Microsoft, to explore how Azure AI Foundry agents can revolutionize DevOps workflows by integrating with GitHub and GitHub Copilot.
In this hands-on demo, Krystal walks through building a security-focused agent using MCP tools, connecting to GitHub's advanced security advisories, and orchestrating multi-agent workflows—all from a local machine. Whether you're a DevOps engineer, security lead, or developer curious about AI-powered automation, this episode is packed with insights to elevate your developer experience.

What You'll Learn:

- How to build a security agent using Azure AI Foundry
- Connecting GitHub MCP tools to automate vulnerability insights
- Using GitHub Copilot to fine-tune prompts for agent instructions
- Running agents locally with secure GitHub token authentication
- Orchestrating multi-agent workflows for DevOps, planning, and documentation
- Leveraging Azure Key Vault and managed identity for secure integrations

videoTitle: Ignition 8.3 Demo: DevOps - Version Control, Deployment Modes, REST API
videoDescription:
Ignition 8.3 introduces powerful DevOps capabilities that make it easier than ever to version, deploy, and manage your Ignition systems. Inductive Automation Chief Technology Evangelist Travis Cox explains how the new file-based configuration model enables seamless Git integration, how Deployment Modes support multiple environment setups, and how the new built-in REST API gives external tools full access to the Ignition Gateway.

FEATURE HIGHLIGHTS
- All Ignition configuration files are now accessible in the file system
- Native Git version control support for projects and gateway configuration
- Environmental overrides with deployment modes
- REST API is now built into Ignition’s gateway web UI
- Full API docs are available via OpenAPI
- Role-based access control for secure API interaction

LIVE DEMO HIGHLIGHTS
- Initializing Git in the data directory and pushing to GitLab
- Viewing JSON project and configuration files directly in Ignition folders
- Making changes and tracking them through GitHub Desktop
- Creating Deployment Modes for dev/test/prod with configuration inheritance
- Viewing mode-specific overrides in Git and the file system
- Using the REST API with tools like Postman and Somnia
- Viewing, adding, and configuring devices and DB connections through the API
- Accessing gateway metrics programmatically through API calls
- Dynamically creating new devices through a POST request

Azure DevOps enables teams to work efficiently together in software development. When combined with InvGate Service Desk, these collaboration capabilities go one step further.

The InvGate Service Management and Azure DevOps integration allows you to link your Azure DevOps Work Items – used to track features, requirements, code bugs, and project development issues – to InvGate Service Desk requests.

- Status of the Work Item in Azure DevOps.

videoTitle: D365 F&O: Setup an Azure DevOps CI Build Pipeline | Part 7
videoDescription:
Set up a CI pipeline for D365 in Azure DevOps. Learn to deploy a Build VM and automate your code builds for team development.

───────────
🕒 Timestamps
───────────

0:00 → Intro: Why We Need a CI Pipeline (Continuous Integration)
0:25 → Part 1: Create a Self-Hosted Agent Pool in ADO
1:19 → Part 2: Provision the Build VM in LCS
1:54 → Selecting the "Build and Test" Topology
2:24 → Advanced Settings: Linking the ADO Agent Pool & Branch
3:04 → Selecting the VM Size (Memory Optimized)
3:47 → Part 3: Configure the Build VM (Reset Password & Connect)
4:24 → Exploring the Build VM File Structure (SDK Folder)
4:53 → Part 4: Review the Auto-Generated Build Pipeline
5:43 → Enabling Continuous Integration Triggers & Path Filters
6:23 → Part 5: Branching Strategy (Rename 'dev' to 'Main')
6:52 → Connecting Visual Studio to Source Control (Team Explorer)
7:19 → Mapping a Local Workspace
7:55 → Adding the Local Project to Source Control
8:34 → Adding the axmodulesbuild File & Checking In
9:12 → Part 6: Creating a New 'dev' Branch from 'Main'
10:06 → Part 7: How the CI Build Process Works
10:23 → Reviewing the Build Stages (Build, Sync, Package)
10:59 → Under the Hood: Pipeline Tasks vs. VM Scripts
11:53 → Part 8: Reviewing the Final Build Artifact
12:27 → Recap

──────────────
💡 What You'll Learn
──────────────

✓ Create a self-hosted Agent Pool in Azure DevOps for D365 builds.
✓ How to deploy a Build VM from LCS and connect it to your pipeline.
✓ Configure an auto-generated build pipeline with CI triggers and path filters.
✓ Connect Visual Studio to Azure DevOps (TFVC) using Team Explorer.
✓ Check in your code to automatically trigger the build and create a Deployable Package.

──────────────────────────
🚀 About This D365 Development Series
──────────────────────────

This series goes beyond basic tutorials to show you how real-world Dynamics 365 (D365FO) projects are built from start to finish. We'll build a complete, end-to-end "Automated Customer Tiering System" for a fictional client, Bluestone Industrial Components (BIC).

You'll see every step of a production-ready solution, from setup to deployment, covering:
➤ Core X++ Development: Extending tables and forms (CustTable), creating Enums, and implementing custom business logic using Chain of Command (CoC).
➤ Automation: Building robust, automated batch jobs with the SysOperation Framework.
➤ APIs & Integration: Creating Data Entities to build secure OData REST APIs and for Data Management Framework (DMF) exports.
➤ Event-Driven Architecture: Firing real-time alerts from D365 to Azure Logic Apps using Business Events.
➤ Business Intelligence: Connecting D365 to Power BI to build an interactive sales dashboard.
➤ DevOps & ALM: Setting up a complete CI/CD pipeline in Azure DevOps, managing source control (TFVC), and automating builds.

────────────────
💻 Tools & Technologies
────────────────

🔹Dynamics 365 Finance & Operations (D365FO)
🔹X++
🔹Visual Studio 2019
🔹Lifecycle Services (LCS)
🔹Azure DevOps (ADO)
🔹SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS)
🔹Power BI Desktop
🔹Azure Logic Apps

───────────────────
💬 Feedback & Future Topics
───────────────────

This series covers the core components of a practical, end-to-end D365 project. I know there are many more topics we could dive into, from advanced debugging to complex form patterns.

I welcome your feedback and ideas! If there's a specific D365 topic you'd like to see covered in a future video, please share your suggestions in the comments section below.

#Dynamics365, #AzureDevOps, #ContinuousIntegration, #D365Development

videoTitle: Create Managed Azure DevOps Pools with Terraform
videoDescription:
Today we're working with Azure Pipelines and exploring the different options you have when working with agent pools. The main focus will be on the new Azure Managed DevOps Agent Pools, what they are, how they work, and why you might want to use them.

We’ll start by deploying some prerequisites using Terraform, including a virtual network to host our agents. You’ll see how to use the AzAPI provider to get the job done and I’ll walk you through it all, step by step, as we build this Terraform project from scratch.

Once everything is up and running, we’ll connect our agent to an Azure Pipeline and run a deployment to test it out.

If you’re looking to automate Azure DevOps agent pool provisioning with Terraform and AzAPI, or just want to learn more about CI/CD in Azure, you’re in the right place. Hope you enjoy the video!

videoTitle: Azure DevOps GitHub Integration with 2-way Sync and Real-Time Updates
videoDescription:
Here's how to quickly set up a secure, seamless 2-way integration that keeps your development teams in sync between Azure DevOps and GitHub. 

00:00 - Use Case
00:30 - Sync Demo
01:30 - Connect to Unito
02:00 - Flow Direction
02:25 - Set Rules and Triggers
02:51 - Linking Fields and Configurations

✨Try it free today:✨
🔗 https://bit.ly/3tAQXYN

Unito supports essential fields in Azure DevOps and GitHub, including:

💼 Title: The title or summary of the issue or work item provides a brief description of what the task or issue entails.

📝 Description: The detailed description of the issue or work item, including any additional information or context needed for team members to understand the task.

👤 Assignee: The person responsible for completing the task or addressing the issue. Keeping the assignee field in sync ensures that the correct team member is aware of their responsibilities.

🔢 Status: The current status of the issue or work item, such as "To Do," "In Progress," or "Done." Syncing the status field helps everyone stay updated on the progress of tasks.

🔝 Priority: The priority level of the issue or work item, which helps determine its importance and urgency. Syncing the priority field ensures that team members are aligned on task prioritization.

📌 Work Item Type/Issue Type: The type of work item or issue, such as bug, feature, or task. Syncing this field provides clarity and consistency in categorizing different types of work.

🏷️ Labels/Tags: Categorizing issues or work items with labels or tags helps with organization and filtering. Keeping these fields in sync ensures consistent labeling across both platforms.

💬 Comments/Updates: Any comments or updates made on an issue or work item should be synced to provide real-time collaboration and visibility for everyone involved.

⏰ Due Date: If there is a specific deadline or due date associated with the task or issue, syncing this field helps the team stay on track and meet deadlines.

📎 Attachments: Any files or attachments related to the issue or work item should be synced to ensure easy access and collaboration.

------------
HELPFUL LINKS

videoTitle: Top 20 Basic Linux Commands Every DevOps Engineer Must Know with Real-World Examples for Beginner
videoDescription:
Basic linux commands tips & tricks. In this beginner-friendly tutorial, I cover 20 basic Linux commands with real-world practical examples, so you can confidently navigate, manage files, and troubleshoot Linux systems — whether you're using Ubuntu, Fedora, Debian, Linux Mint, or any other Linux distros. Whether you're a DevOps engineer, sysadmin, Linux beginner, or IT student, this hands-on tutorial will boost your terminal skills and productivity!

Perfect for:
- Beginners exploring Linux for the first time
- Students preparing for certifications like Linux+, RHCSA, or LPIC
- DevOps and System Admins who want to brush up on daily-use commands

------------------------------------------------------------
           Timestamps / Chapters: 
------------------------------------------------------------
00:00 Introduction to Basic Linux Commands for Beginners
00:30 *uname* = Check Linux System Info using uname Command
00:57 *whoami* = How to Find Logged In User in Linux with whoami
01:10 *pwd* = Get Current Directory Path in Linux using pwd
01:26 *mkdir* = Create New Directory in Linux using mkdir
01:35 *ls* = List Files and Directories in Linux with ls Command
01:44 *touch* = Create a New File from Terminal using touch Command
01:56 *echo* = Add Text to a File in Linux using echo Command
02:10 *cat* = Display File Content in Terminal using cat Command
02:19 *rm* = Delete Files or Directories in Linux using rm Command
02:41 *clear* = Clear the Terminal Screen in Linux with clear Command
02:52 *ping* = Check Internet Connection in Linux using ping
03:12 *curl* = Fetch URL or API Response in Linux using curl
03:29 *cd* = Change Directory in Linux using cd Command
03:51 *cp* = Copy Files and Directories in Linux using cp Command
04:14 *mv* = Move or Rename Files in Linux using mv Command
04:36 *ls -l* = View File Details and Permissions in Linux with ls -l
04:54 *nano* = Edit Files in Terminal using nano Text Editor
05:23 *chown* = Change File Ownership in Linux with chown Command
05:46 *chmod* = Modify File Permissions in Linux using chmod Command

videoTitle: Manage & Secure Kubernetes Cluster Policies via OPA Gatekeeper. #opa #devops #gatekeeper
videoDescription:
Manage & Secure Kubernetes Cluster Policies via OPA Gatekeeper. #opa #devops #gatekeepers 

Timestamps:

Theory/Concepts : 00:00
Hands on/Practical : 09:00

github repo : https://github.com/RohanRusta21/opa-gatekeeper.git

Concepts :

OPA Gatekeeper: Policy and Governance for Kubernetes

What is OPA:

The Open Policy Agent (OPA) is an open-source, general-purpose policy engine that unifies policy enforcement across the stack. OPA provides a high-level declarative language that lets us specify policies as code and simple APIs to offload policy decision-making from our software. We can use OPA to enforce policies in microservices, Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines, API gateways, and more. 
In kubernetes, OPA uses admission controllers.

What is OPA Gatekeeper?

OPA Gatekeeper is a specialized project providing first-class integration between OPA and Kubernetes.
OPA Gatekeeper adds the following on top of plain OPA:
● An extensible, parameterized policy library.
● Native Kubernetes CRDs for instantiating the policy library (aka “constraints”).
● Native Kubernetes CRDs for extending the policy library (aka “constraint templates”).
● Audit functionality.

Why you need Gatekeeper?

As a Platform DevOps engineer managing multiple Kubernetes clusters for various projects, you face challenges when users deploy applications without adhering to best practices. Key issues include:
  1.     Lack of ownership unless users apply labels themselves.
  2.     No resource limits, leading to high costs.
  3.     Untrusted images impacting other applications.
  4.     Security and best practice violations across clusters.

To address these, we need governance to enforce limits and rules via policies. OPA Gatekeeper can be configured to implement these policies, ensuring best practices are followed.

🔹 Generates an SBOM with Syft

videoTitle: What is Git? How would you define Git? || #GitHub #GitLab #BitBucket #DevOps #Git #Python #Java
videoDescription:
What is Git?

How would you define Git?

Well, Git is a distributed version control system, which means two things:

1. It's distributed so people around the world can share code
2. It does version control

So, with version control, you can take snapshots of the state of your current code and maintain a history.

Branching is really the third thing. Git branches allow developers to do isolated development and take chances without messing up the main branch.

So when someone asks me 'What is Git?', that is how I typically respond.

videoDescription:

Don't forget to like, share, and subscribe! Hit the bell icon to stay updated with our latest videos.

For Interview related training please feel free to reach us over the call 9845421738 to discuss further.

I am gonna tell you what DevOps engineers do in less than a minute with an example.

In a restaurant kitchen, there are chefs and wait staff.

Chefs are responsible for creating and experimenting with new dishes. Their job includes coming up with innovative recipes, testing them, and adding the successful ones to the menu. At the same time, they also prepare food to meet the regular demand of customers who visit the restaurant daily. This dual responsibility of innovation and ongoing service can become overwhelming, especially during busy hours when the kitchen is already at full capacity.

Wait staff, on the other hand, focus on delivering the prepared food to customers. Their primary role is to ensure that the food reaches the diners promptly and in perfect condition, keeping the restaurant’s operations running smoothly.

Experimenting with a new dish during peak hours, however, becomes a significant challenge. The process of testing new recipes while maintaining the flow of regular orders often leads to disruptions in the kitchen, causing delays and dissatisfaction among customers.

To address this, the restaurant hires a kitchen manager. The kitchen manager creates a separate small kitchen dedicated to experimenting with new dishes. This ensures that the chefs can focus on their innovations without interfering with the ongoing operations. Once a dish is perfected, the kitchen manager introduces automation, such as using a conveyor belt, to streamline the cooking process. This allows the new dish to be produced efficiently alongside the existing menu items while ensuring there are no interruptions in serving food to the restaurant's customers.

Yes, DevOps engineers are none other than automation experts.

videoTitle: RabbitMQ for DevOps Engineers: Hands-On Demo for Beginners #devops #rabbitmq  #kubernetes
videoDescription:
RabbitMQ for DevOps Engineers: Hands-On Demo for Beginners #devops #rabbitmq  #kubernetes

Github Repo : https://github.com/RohanRusta21/demo_rabbitmq.git

RabbitMQ Official Doc : https://www.rabbitmq.com/docs

Timestamp:
Understand Concept : 00:00
RabbitMQ Hands-on : 22:06

🧠 1. What Is RabbitMQ?

RabbitMQ is an open-source message broker — it acts as a middleman between different parts of a system that need to communicate asynchronously.
It uses the AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) as its core protocol.

In simpler terms:

It receives messages from producers (applications sending data),

Stores them temporarily in queues,

And delivers them to consumers (applications receiving data).

This helps decouple systems — the sender and receiver don’t need to be active at the same time.

⚙️ 2. Why Use RabbitMQ?

✅ Decoupling – Producers don’t need to know who consumes messages.
✅ Asynchronous communication – Improves system responsiveness.
✅ Load balancing – Messages can be distributed across multiple consumers.
✅ Reliability – Ensures message delivery even if parts of the system fail.
✅ Scalability – You can add more queues or consumers easily.
✅ Flexible routing – Decide how messages flow using exchanges and routing keys.

🔩 3. Core RabbitMQ Concepts
🏭 Producers

Applications that send messages to RabbitMQ.
They don’t send messages directly to a queue but to an exchange.

🎯 Exchanges

An exchange routes messages to queues based on routing rules.

Exchange types:

Type Behavior Example Use Case
Direct Routes messages to queues whose binding key exactly matches routing key Task queues
Fanout Broadcasts all messages to all queues bound to it Notifications, pub/sub
Topic Routes messages based on pattern matching (* and #) Selective subscriptions
Headers Routes based on header attributes Advanced filtering

videoTitle: What's the goal of Platform Engineering? #shorts #devops #platformengineering
videoDescription:
Watch the full video: https://youtu.be/JAblex0uS4k

What’s the goal of Platform Engineering? Platform Engineering focusses on the building the infrastructure and the systems that are needed for running your applications and supporting your application throughout the software life cycle. So,  the goal  is to support the developers in the organisation and treat them as customers. That’s why we have in platform engineering the concept of IDP Internal developer platform.
 
So, this platform is treated as an actual product and the customers of the product are the developers.
What is the role of the IDP?
It is to create a thin layer on top of the toolchain that is used inside the organisation to standardise the processes and help developers to have one way to deploy into production

videoTitle: How to Fix High CPU Usage in Production | DevOps Real-Time Scenario (Day 3)
videoDescription:
💥 Day 3 of the DevOps in ARIA Series – Real-Time Scenarios!

🎯 Today’s Interview Question:
“Your production server is showing high CPU usage — what will you do?”

This is more than an interview question — it’s a real-world DevOps challenge you’ll face on the job.

🎥 In this video, I break down:

✅ How to investigate CPU issues using tools like top, htop, Grafana, and Prometheus

✅ How to identify which process or service is consuming resources

✅ When to restart a service — and what to check before and after

✅ How code bugs or deployment issues can spike CPU

✅ When to scale infra or adjust auto-scaling policies

✅ Why documenting and learning from incidents makes you a stronger engineer

💡 Clarity, calmness, and a systematic approach — that’s the DevOps way!

📌 If you're preparing for DevOps interviews or working in production environments, this video will sharpen your troubleshooting skills.

🔗 Watch now, comment your thoughts, and don’t forget to subscribe for Day 4 and beyond!

videoTitle: Linux Commands That DevOps Engineers Use Every Single Day
videoDescription:
Welcome back to the Linux for DevOps Engineers playlist!

In this hands-on session, we focus on the essential Linux commands used daily by DevOps, SRE, Cloud, and Kubernetes engineers.

This is not a command memorisation tutorial; it's real, practical understanding.
Linux Essential Commands | Linux for DevOps Engineers
In this session, you will learn:

✅ How to know where you are in Linux (pwd, ls)
✅ How to navigate directories using cd (absolute + relative paths)
✅ How to create files & folders using mkdir, echo, cat
✅ How to find files with find
✅ How to search inside files using grep
✅ How to check system info, kernel version & disk health (uname, df -h, du -sh)
✅ How to monitor live logs using tail -f
✅ How to view system service logs using journalctl
✅ How to edit files with vi/vim without getting stuck
✅ A practice task to build confidence

This video is designed for complete beginners, DevOps learners, and anyone preparing for Linux + Kubernetes work.

videoTitle: How we Use DevOps in Tableau   the Coffee Chat
videoDescription:
Harnessing DevOps Principles with Tableau at GitLab: A Deep Dive

Join Peter and I as we talk about how we took inspiration from DevOps principles as we migrated into Tableau (as an enterprise). We developed our Tableau workflows to enhance trust, flexibility, and scalability.

We discuss how we incorporated these DevOps principles at GitLab, including change management, code reviews, and custom tools that automate administrative tasks. 

We also share our DevOps friendly strategies for stakeholder communication, maintaining version control, and ensuring successful dashboard adoption. Interactive Q&A sessions address common challenges and provide practical tips for professionals looking to implement similar methods in their organizations.

videoTitle: I Tried Learning DevOps Only From Documentation — Most People Would Quit
videoDescription:
Everyone tells you to watch tutorials to learn DevOps.

That’s not how I learned it.

In this video, I share my real DevOps learning method — exactly how I used to learn tools when I was starting out:
• Opening official documentation
• Following it step by step
• Breaking things
• Fixing real errors
• Learning how engineers actually think

This is a raw 1-hour session using Jenkins official documentation — no courses, no scripts, no shortcuts, no AI.

You’ll see:
• How beginners actually get stuck
• What documentation explains well (and what it doesn’t)
• Why pipelines fail even when you “follow the steps”
• How real DevOps engineers debug without answers

⚠️ This is NOT a polished tutorial
⚠️ This is NOT a crash course
✅ This is real DevOps learning in action

If you’re serious about learning DevOps, CI/CD, or Jenkins, this mindset matters more than tools.

💬 Comment: How did YOU learn DevOps — tutorials or documentation?

videoTitle: Hello World – DevOps Breakdown Song | Screams, Bugs & Git Crimes

- Git reset hard scream therapy

- Kevin... just Kevin.

Lyrics
=====

hello... heLLo... HELLO WORLD?  
Can anybody hear me behind the firewall...?  
404 soul not found...

Push to prod at 3 A.M.,

videoTitle: Day 29: Monitoring & Logging in Azure DevOps | 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge 🚀
videoDescription:
👋 Welcome to Day 29 of my 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge 🚀

Today’s focus: Monitoring & Logging in Azure DevOps 🔍

Monitoring and logging are the backbone of observability in DevOps. While automation ensures smooth deployments, monitoring ensures that systems stay healthy, and logging helps you catch errors before they become big problems.

🔹 What we covered today:

Why monitoring & logging are critical in DevOps

Difference between Monitoring (health & performance) and Logging (detailed events & errors)

Using Azure DevOps pipeline logs to debug failures

Dashboards & reports for performance tracking

Integration with Azure Monitor and Application Insights for full observability

✨ Key takeaway:
Automation doesn’t stop at deployment — it continues with monitoring, logging, and observability for reliable DevOps.

📌 Next in Day 30 → Security & Compliance in Azure DevOps 🔒🚀

👍 Don’t forget to Like, Comment, and Subscribe to follow along with the full 30 Days Challenge!

Using the Pulumi platform, teams can build, deploy, and manage modern cloud applications faster and with more confidence, using any language, any architecture and any cloud. 

Pulumi lets teams build Universal Infrastructure as Code using popular programming languages (Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, .NET/C#, Java) and markup languages (YAML, JSON, CUE). It enables deploying infrastructure and applications together through a unified delivery process. Finally, teams can manage cloud applications with Policy as Code, better visibility, and controls.

videoTitle: Git for IBM i: Enterprise DevOps Without the Enterprise Pain
videoDescription:
Corporate mandates Git. You’ve seen how Git makes it easy to view the history of changes and how it enables the productivity of parallel development. Your .NET, Java, and Python teams work in GitLab, GitHub, or Azure DevOps. Yet your IBM i team is still locked in proprietary tools that lack the visibility provided by Git while slowing development down with single-developer code locks. They watch as their peers deliver features twice as fast using parallel development workflows they can’t access.

The frustration is real: developers waste hours waiting for code access, recruiters struggle to find talent willing to work with legacy tools, and CIOs face the impossible choice between IBM i support and advanced productivity. But you CAN have it all. In this webinar, we will show you how you can take advantage of ALL the features of Git-based DevOps while still handling the unique requirements of the IBM i environment. While many IBM i DevOps tools offer “Git integration,” in which you work with a legacy DevOps tool that “talks” to Git, this webinar will show how IBM i developers can work directly with Git.

We will demonstrate the only solution that lets IBM i developers actually work in Git: the same repositories, the same workflows, the same tools as every other developer in your organization. See live examples of parallel development eliminating code lock bottlenecks, IBM i code managed in GitLab with automated CI/CD pipelines, and teams accelerating feature delivery by breaking free from the barriers that have held them back for decades.

Join Eradani’s CEO, Dan Magid, and Chief Architect, Aaron Magid, to discover how organizations solve the problems nobody else can solve: enabling true parallel development, recruiting modern developers to IBM i teams, and finally achieving the enterprise DevOps compliance your auditors demand. Plus, see how Git AI integration (including GitHub Copilot) accelerates IBM i development without replacing your existing RPG expertise.

Learn more at www.eradani.com.

videoTitle: Stop using Read-Host and do this instead #powershell  #devops  #scripting
videoDescription:
Stop using Read-Host and do this instead:

Read-Host has limitations. 

- You cannot pass information to it 
- You cannot validate what the user provided
- It always stops the script

There is a better way - use parameters instead.

- Control the type of input
- Make it mandatory or optional
- Pass argument from the command line
- or prompt the user to provide an argument, when required and not passed

In this short video, I'll show you how to implement simple parameters and how to use them.

Would you like to learn more about the parameters?

videoTitle: Bash Scripting for DevOps Engineers | Automate Kubernetes, Disk & Log Monitoring
videoDescription:
Bash scripting is one of the most powerful skills every DevOps engineer should know.

In real DevOps environments, engineers repeatedly check things like:
• Is the Kubernetes pod running?
• Is the disk almost full?
• Are there errors in system logs?

Doing this manually once is fine — but doing it every day is not scalable.

This is where bash scripting helps automate repetitive operational tasks.

In this tutorial, you’ll learn the fundamentals of shell scripting and then apply them to real DevOps automation examples used in production environments.

🔧 What You’ll Learn

• How to create and execute a shell script
• Understanding the shebang (#!/bin/bash)
• Setting executable permissions with chmod +x
• Using variables in bash scripting
• Writing if-else conditions for automation decisions
• Using for loops in shell scripts
• Using exit codes for automation workflows

🚀 Real DevOps Automation Scripts

✔ Kubernetes Pod Health Check Script (kubectl + JSONPath)
✔ Disk Usage Monitoring Script with threshold alerts (df, awk, sed)
✔ Real-time Log Monitoring Script using tail -f and grep

These examples demonstrate how small scripts can automate daily DevOps operations like monitoring services, detecting failures, and preventing production issues.

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Why DevOps Engineers Automate Repetitive Tasks
00:34 - How to Create and Run Your First Shell Script
02:15 - Using Variables in Shell Scripts
03:26 - If Conditions in Bash (Automation Decisions)
06:48 - For Loops in Shell Scripts
08:58 - From Concepts to Real DevOps Automation
09:30 - Script #1: Check Kubernetes Pod Health
12:35 - Script #2: Monitor Disk Usage Automatically
16:09 - Script #3: Detect Errors in Log Files
18:44 - How Small Scripts Power DevOps Automation
19:06 - DevOps Mindset: When to Automate Commands

📌 If you're learning DevOps, Kubernetes, or Linux automation, mastering bash scripting is one of the most valuable skills you can develop.

Subscribe for more tutorials on DevOps, Kubernetes, Linux, and Cloud automation.

videoTitle: Linux Package Management for DevOps: Stop Breaking Your Deployments
videoDescription:
Linux Package Management for DevOps: Stop Breaking Your Deployments

Package managers are not exciting. But if you’re a DevOps or Kubernetes engineer, they are absolutely foundational.

In this video, you’ll learn Linux package management the way it really matters in production as an ongoing operational responsibility, not a one-time setup task. We’ll connect tools like yum, apt and dnf directly to Ansible, CI/CD pipelines and Kubernetes nodes so you actually understand what you’re automating.

What you’ll learn:
00:00 - Why DevOps engineers must understand package managers
01:01 - Package Management in Real Cloud & Enterprise Environments
01:19 - How DevOps Engineers Actually Use Package Managers (Reality Check)
01:29 - What You’ll Learn in This Video (Scope & Expectations)
01:59 - What Is a Package Manager? (Concept, Not Commands)
02:47 - Where Yum Gets Packages From & What It Really Does
03:15 - RHEL Subscription Manager Explained (Enterprise Reality)
05:33 - When You Don’t Use Subscription Manager (Air-gapped & Internal Repos)
05:52 - Yum Repository Configuration Explained (/etc/yum.repos.d)
07:32 - Core Yum Commands DevOps Engineers Use Daily
11:15 - Production Updates: What NOT to Do & How Updates Really Work
12:14 - Why Blind yum update -y Breaks Production
12:52 - Package Managers Across Distros (Yum vs Apt vs Zypper)
13:37 - Final Takeaway: Why This Layer Matters for Kubernetes & Automation

By the end, you’ll be able to:
- Understand what yum/apt/dnf are doing under your automation tools
- Avoid “works on my machine” package issues in CI/CD
- Debug broken Kubernetes nodes at the Linux layer
- Treat package management as a continuous DevOps responsibility

This video is part of my “Linux for DevOps: From Zero to Hired” series, designed for:
- DevOps engineers
- SREs and Platform engineers
- Developers moving into Kubernetes and cloud-native roles

Next step after package management: learn Linux networking basics, so Kubernetes networking finally makes sense.

If this helped, subscribe for more Linux for DevOps content and Kubernetes-focused breakdowns.

videoTitle: Is DevOps Dead? The Truth in 26 Seconds #shorts
videoDescription:
People say DevOps is dying because of AI.

Not really.

DevOps was never just a role. It’s how teams build, deploy, and run software together.

The tools are changing, AI is entering the workflow, but DevOps itself isn’t disappearing.

If you work in cloud or engineering, this shift matters.

#devops #devopsengineer #ai #cloudcomputing #kubernetes #platformengineering #softwareengineering #automation #programming #tech
channelDescription:
Most infrastructure advice works in controlled demos. It often fails under real constraints.

SystemAxis explores how AI, cloud, and DevOps systems behave in production — under load, under pressure, and under real cost limits.

This channel focuses on practical engineering: stress testing configurations, analyzing bottlenecks, understanding failure modes, and making deliberate architectural decisions. Sometimes that means running controlled experiments. Sometimes it means breaking assumptions. Sometimes it means designing systems properly from the start.

Topics include AI infrastructure, cloud architecture, Linux internals, automation, cost control, platform engineering, and performance tuning.

The goal is simple: understand how systems actually behave - not how they are supposed to behave.

No hype. No demo-only advice. Just production reality.

videoTitle: Linux Networking Tools for DevOps: curl, wget, dig & traceroute Explained with Real Demos
videoDescription:
Linux Networking Tools for DevOps: curl, wget, dig & traceroute Explained with Real Demos

In the last video, we looked at how Linux networking works internally.
In this video, we’re going fully practical: the exact tools DevOps engineers use during real production incidents.

You won’t just “learn commands,” you’ll learn what question each tool answers:
• curl – is the service actually responding?
• wget – can I download content / check connectivity quickly?
• dig – does this DNS name resolve to an IP address?
• traceroute – where does the network path break?

By the end, you’ll have a simple 4-step flow you can run every time something is “just not working” in production.

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps  ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Why Debugging Matters More Than Networking Theory
00:37 - curl: Is the Service Actually Responding?
02:34 - wget: Download & Connectivity Check
03:24 - curl vs wget: Which One Should You Use?
03:50 - dig: Does DNS Resolve or Not?
04:34 - traceroute: Where Does Traffic Stop?
05:53 - Real Debugging Flow + What’s Next

What you’ll learn:
• How to use curl to validate services, ports, and status codes
• When wget is better than curl in CI/CD and scripts
• How dig helps you separate DNS problems from app problems
• How traceroute reveals where packets stop on the network path
• A practical, repeatable debug order for real incidents

channelDescription:
Stop answering like a Junior. Start getting Senior offers.

Most candidates fail technical interviews not because they don’t know the tools —
they fail because they collapse when the scenario changes.

"ACE INTERVIEWS"  is the channel for experienced tech professionals who want to win Senior, Lead, and Architect interviews by mastering real-world production scenarios — the same ones interviewers use to separate the top 10% from everyone else.

We do not teach features.
We teach decision-making under pressure.

Whether you work in SAP, Salesforce, DevOps, Software Testing, Cloud, Data, Cybersecurity, or Software Engineering, your interview always comes down to:

1.What breaks?
2. What do you fix first?
3. What trade-off do you choose?

videoTitle: SRE, DevOps, DevSecOps, Cloud Engineering, and Platform Engineering - What's the difference???
videoDescription:
The key differences between SRE, DevOps, DevSecOps, Cloud Engineering, and Platform Engineering are explained in this comprehensive video! Are you confused about these popular tech roles and methodologies? We break down each concept, explaining its core principles, responsibilities, and how they relate to one another.

In this video, you'll learn:

* **Site Reliability Engineering (SRE):** Focus on reliability, performance, and efficiency of production systems using software engineering principles.
* **DevOps:** A culture and set of practices that aims to break down silos between Development and Operations teams, fostering collaboration and automation.
* **DevSecOps:** Integrating security practices into every stage of the DevOps lifecycle, making security a shared responsibility.
* **Cloud Engineering:** Designing, building, and managing infrastructure and applications on cloud platforms like AWS, Azure, and GCP.
* **Platform Engineering:** Building and maintaining internal developer platforms to streamline software delivery and enhance developer experience.

Whether you're a student, a tech professional looking to specialise, or simply curious about these in-demand fields, this video provides a clear and concise explanation to help you understand the nuances and overlaps between SRE, DevOps, DevSecOps, Cloud Engineering, and Platform Engineering.

Don't forget to like, comment, and subscribe for more tech explainers!

videoTitle: The DevOps Trap Nobody Talks About #shorts
videoDescription:
Most DevOps engineers get noticed only when things break. That work matters — but it rarely gets rewarded.

The real career advantage comes from preventing problems before anyone sees them. Quiet prevention beats loud firefighting.

How to move from Junior to Senior DevOps Thinking:

Master Infrastructure Stability over simple uptime.

Focus on Risk Management and automation.

Build for SRE (Site Reliability Engineering) standards.

#DevOps #CloudEngineering #SRE #EngineeringCareers #TechLeadership #SoftwareEngineering #SystemAxis #SeniorDevOps
channelDescription:
Most infrastructure advice works in controlled demos. It often fails under real constraints.

SystemAxis explores how AI, cloud, and DevOps systems behave in production — under load, under pressure, and under real cost limits.

This channel focuses on practical engineering: stress testing configurations, analyzing bottlenecks, understanding failure modes, and making deliberate architectural decisions. Sometimes that means running controlled experiments. Sometimes it means breaking assumptions. Sometimes it means designing systems properly from the start.

Topics include AI infrastructure, cloud architecture, Linux internals, automation, cost control, platform engineering, and performance tuning.

The goal is simple: understand how systems actually behave - not how they are supposed to behave.

No hype. No demo-only advice. Just production reality.

videoTitle: Build an AI PR Reviewer with GitHub Actions (DevOps Tutorial - AWS Bedrock + Terraform)
videoDescription:
0:00 Intro – Why DevOps Needs AI Now
0:40 Demo – AI Reviewing a Pull Request with AWS Bedrock
1:08 Environment Setup (Terraform, AWS, GitHub, VS Code)
1:55 Creating the GitHub Repo with Terraform
3:12 Terraform Init & First Apply
3:53 Project Structure (infra/ + scripts/)
6:21 Creating S3 Backend & DynamoDB State Lock
8:21 Setting Up GitHub OIDC in AWS IAM
11:39 Terraform Plan & Apply (IAM + Bedrock Role)
12:26 Building the GitHub Actions Workflow
15:42 Writing the Python Bedrock Review Script
20:51 Testing the PR Workflow
21:58 Fixing “No Merge Base” GitHub Error
23:34 Debugging Bedrock Model Issues (Claude EOL)
24:40 Switching to Nova 2 Light Model
25:44 Manual Bedrock API Fix (Inference Config)
30:28 Improving Human-Readable Output
33:31 Successful AI PR Review Demo
33:50 Testing AI Against Insecure EC2 Example
35:50 AI Security Findings & Best Practice Feedback
36:47 Final Thoughts – AI Won’t Replace You (But You Must Adapt)

In this video, I build an AI-powered DevOps pull request reviewer using GitHub Actions, AWS Bedrock, and Terraform.

Instead of relying on manual code reviews, this pipeline automatically:

Assumes an AWS IAM role using OIDC (no static keys)

Extracts the pull request diff

Sends the changes to an AI model in AWS Bedrock

Posts a structured DevOps review directly on the PR

We’ll cover:

Terraform remote state best practices

GitHub OIDC federation with AWS

IAM trust policies

Bedrock model invocation

Automating infrastructure reviews with AI

This is what modern AI-integrated DevOps pipelines look like.

If you're a DevOps engineer, platform engineer, or cloud engineer looking to integrate AI into CI/CD, this build will give you a practical starting point.

videoTitle: Linux Firewall Explained for DevOps | firewalld, iptables & Troubleshooting
videoDescription:
Linux Firewall Explained for DevOps | firewalld, iptables & Troubleshooting

Firewall issues are one of the most common causes of production outages in DevOps. Your service is running, DNS works, but traffic still fails. In many cases, the Linux firewall is the root cause.

In this video, you will learn how Linux firewall works using firewalld and iptables, and how packet filtering happens inside the Linux kernel.

This tutorial focuses on real DevOps troubleshooting using practical commands.

Topics covered:

• How Linux firewall packet filtering works
• firewalld explained with real examples
• How to check firewall status and zones
• How to allow ports using firewall-cmd
• iptables explained for DevOps engineers
• INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD chains explained
• How Kubernetes uses iptables internally
• DevOps firewall troubleshooting workflow

This video is part of the Linux for DevOps series.

If you want to become strong in Docker, Kubernetes, and DevOps, understanding Linux firewall is essential.

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps  ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Introduction: Why Firewall Issues Break DevOps Systems
00:33 - How Linux Firewall Packet Filtering Works (Basic Concept)
01:16 - firewalld Tutorial: Check Status, Zones, and Allow Ports
03:00 - iptables Explained: How Linux Actually Filters Packets
03:44 - INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD Chains Explained (Visual Guide)
04:18 - DevOps Firewall Troubleshooting Mindset + Outro

videoTitle: Top 5 IT Domains in 2025|AI|ML|Cybersecurity|Cloud|DevOps|Data|FullStack Development
videoDescription:
On this channel, we will dive deep into the fascinating worlds of DevOps and Cloud. Together, we will explore the powerful fusion of development and operations, enabling organizations to deliver software faster, more reliably, and at scale. I will share my knowledge, insights, and practical experiences to help you navigate the ever-evolving landscape of DevOps and Cloud.

1. Artificial Intelligence (AI) & Machine Learning

 Technologies/Skills to Learn:
  Python, TensorFlow, PyTorch
  OpenAI API, LangChain, Hugging Face
  Scikit-learn, Pandas, NumPy
  MLflow, Weights & Biases (MLOps)
  Prompt Engineering (for GenAI)
  LLMs (GPT, Claude, Gemini)

 Roles:
  AI/ML Engineer
  Data Scientist
  GenAI Prompt Engineer
  AI Product Manager

2. Cybersecurity

 Technologies/Skills to Learn:
  Network Security, Firewalls (e.g., Palo Alto, Fortinet)
  SIEM tools (Splunk, ELK, QRadar)
  Identity & Access Management (IAM, Okta)
  Zero Trust Security Model
  Cloud Security (AWS Security Hub, Azure Defender)
  Ethical Hacking, Penetration Testing

 Roles:
  Security Analyst
  Cybersecurity Engineer
  SOC Analyst
  Cloud Security Architect

3. Cloud Computing & DevOps

 Technologies/Skills to Learn:
  Cloud Platforms: AWS, Azure, GCP
  Docker, Kubernetes, Helm
  Terraform, Pulumi (Infrastructure as Code)
  Jenkins, GitHub Actions, ArgoCD (CI/CD)
  Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana, ELK
  Linux, Bash, YAML

 Roles:
  Cloud Engineer / Architect
  DevOps Engineer
  Site Reliability Engineer (SRE)
  Platform Engineer

4. Data Engineering & nalytics

 Technologies/Skills to Learn:
  SQL, Python (Pandas, NumPy)
  Apache Spark, Apache Kafka
  Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift
  Airflow, dbt (Data Orchestration)
  Power BI, Tableau, Looker
  Data Lake, Delta Lake, Data Warehousing

 Roles:
  Data Engineer
  BI Analyst
  DataOps Engineer
  Analytics Engineer

5. Full Stack Development & App Engineering

 Technologies/Skills to Learn:
  Frontend: React.js, Next.js, Angular
  Backend: Node.js, Spring Boot, Django
  REST & GraphQL APIs
  JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Python
  CI/CD, Docker, Testing frameworks (Jest, JUnit)
  Databases: PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis

 Roles:
  Full Stack Developer
  Frontend Developer
  Backend Developer
  App Developer
  Technical Lead

videoTitle: Introduction to Secure DevOps | AZ-400 | Episode 22
videoDescription:
Episode 22 of 25
For the full video series, click here: https://aka.ms/AZ-400onYouTube

In this episode, you’ll explore DevSecOps and learn how modern DevOps practices integrate security and compliance into every stage of the software development lifecycle. The session examines why security is a shared responsibility, reviews common threats such as SQL injection, and explains how to build secure pipelines with continuous validation.

You’ll also learn how tools such as CodeQL, threat modeling, and automated scanning help identify vulnerabilities early in the development process and reduce risk. The episode concludes with an overview of open-source software, associated risks, and strategies for managing licensing and compliance. This content aligns with AZ-400 certification objectives related to secure development and compliance practices.

Learn more about this course and take the certification exam to test your new skills: https://aka.ms/AZ-400onLearn

videoTitle: How to Fix High CPU Usage in Production | DevOps Real-Time Scenario (Day 3)
videoDescription:
💥 Day 3 of the DevOps in ARIA Series – Real-Time Scenarios!

🎯 Today’s Interview Question:
“Your production server is showing high CPU usage — what will you do?”

This is more than an interview question — it’s a real-world DevOps challenge you’ll face on the job.

🎥 In this video, I break down:

✅ How to investigate CPU issues using tools like top, htop, Grafana, and Prometheus

✅ How to identify which process or service is consuming resources

✅ When to restart a service — and what to check before and after

✅ How code bugs or deployment issues can spike CPU

✅ When to scale infra or adjust auto-scaling policies

✅ Why documenting and learning from incidents makes you a stronger engineer

💡 Clarity, calmness, and a systematic approach — that’s the DevOps way!

📌 If you're preparing for DevOps interviews or working in production environments, this video will sharpen your troubleshooting skills.

🔗 Watch now, comment your thoughts, and don’t forget to subscribe for Day 4 and beyond!

videoTitle: Linux Commands That DevOps Engineers Use Every Single Day
videoDescription:
Welcome back to the Linux for DevOps Engineers playlist!

In this hands-on session, we focus on the essential Linux commands used daily by DevOps, SRE, Cloud, and Kubernetes engineers.

This is not a command memorisation tutorial; it's real, practical understanding.
Linux Essential Commands | Linux for DevOps Engineers
In this session, you will learn:

✅ How to know where you are in Linux (pwd, ls)
✅ How to navigate directories using cd (absolute + relative paths)
✅ How to create files & folders using mkdir, echo, cat
✅ How to find files with find
✅ How to search inside files using grep
✅ How to check system info, kernel version & disk health (uname, df -h, du -sh)
✅ How to monitor live logs using tail -f
✅ How to view system service logs using journalctl
✅ How to edit files with vi/vim without getting stuck
✅ A practice task to build confidence

This video is designed for complete beginners, DevOps learners, and anyone preparing for Linux + Kubernetes work.

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps ⏰  ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Introduction
00:33 - Understanding PWD & LS Commands (View Location + List Files)
02:12 - Navigating Linux with CD (Relative & Absolute Paths)  
03:43 - Creating Files & Folders + Using CAT & ECHO 
05:20 – Finding Files in Linux with the FIND Command 
07:33 - Searching Text Inside Files Using GREP  
09:41 - Checking System Info, Kernel, Disk Usage & Health (uname, df, du) 
12:49 - Live Log Monitoring with Tail -f  
14:11 - Viewing System Service Logs Using journalctl 
15:52 - Getting Started with VI/Vim Editor (Absolute Basics) 
18:18 - Hands-on Practice Task for You  
19:30 - Conclusion & Next Steps

📌 Practice Task (Do This!)
Create a folder, add files, insert content, search using grep, locate files with find, check folder size, and monitor logs using tail.
Share your commands in the comments to help others learn!

videoTitle: Automating Azure DevOps (ADO) to SharePoint with Power Automate: Data Shuttle, Smartsheet & ADO Link
videoDescription: This video demonstrates a powerful Power Automate connector designed to integrate with Azure DevOps (ADO). Learn how to automate the process of pulling work items from ADO, generating a CSV file, and placing it into a SharePoint folder for Data Shuttle to process. The workflow includes archiving old files and ensuring only the latest data is available. See a live test showcasing how updates, like "Brooke test take two" and "Yuriy test one," are reflected in the new CSV, complete with direct URL links to specific ADO work items. We also explore future possibilities, including potentially replacing the existing Software connector, pushing data directly to Smartsheet with hyperlinks and hierarchy, and achieving bidirectional integration.

videoTitle: Day 29: Monitoring & Logging in Azure DevOps | 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge 🚀
videoDescription:
👋 Welcome to Day 29 of my 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge 🚀

Today’s focus: Monitoring & Logging in Azure DevOps 🔍

Monitoring and logging are the backbone of observability in DevOps. While automation ensures smooth deployments, monitoring ensures that systems stay healthy, and logging helps you catch errors before they become big problems.

🔹 What we covered today:

Why monitoring & logging are critical in DevOps

Difference between Monitoring (health & performance) and Logging (detailed events & errors)

Using Azure DevOps pipeline logs to debug failures

Dashboards & reports for performance tracking

Integration with Azure Monitor and Application Insights for full observability

✨ Key takeaway:
Automation doesn’t stop at deployment — it continues with monitoring, logging, and observability for reliable DevOps.

📌 Next in Day 30 → Security & Compliance in Azure DevOps 🔒🚀

videoTitle: 99% of DevOps Engineers Don’t Understand This Networking Flow (End-to-End Explained)
videoDescription:
Every time you type a website URL and press Enter, an invisible journey begins.

But…
👉 Do you really know what happens behind the scenes?
👉 How does your request travel from a browser to a Kubernetes pod?
👉 Why do failures happen even when “everything looks fine”?

In this video, we trace ONE single web request end-to-end and break down networking in a way that finally makes sense — without boring theory.

🔍 What You’ll Learn in This Video

✔ What DNS actually does (and why most outages start here)
✔ How HTTPS works and why TLS handshakes matter
✔ TCP/IP explained from a real debugging perspective
✔ Firewalls, ports, and security groups — simplified
✔ Load balancers and how they protect systems from failure
✔ Kubernetes networking: Ingress, Services, and Pods
✔ How to debug production issues layer by layer

This is not just networking theory — this is real-world DevOps networking, explained using stories, visuals, and practical insights.

👨‍💻 Who This Video Is For

DevOps Engineers

Cloud Engineers (AWS / GCP / Azure)

Kubernetes beginners & professionals

Backend developers

Anyone preparing for interviews or real production work

🧠 Why This Video Is Different

Most tutorials teach networking in silos.
This video teaches the entire journey of a request, the way production systems actually work.

“If you can understand one request deeply, you can debug any system.”

videoTitle: Linux Package Management for DevOps: Stop Breaking Your Deployments
videoDescription:
Linux Package Management for DevOps: Stop Breaking Your Deployments

Package managers are not exciting. But if you’re a DevOps or Kubernetes engineer, they are absolutely foundational.

In this video, you’ll learn Linux package management the way it really matters in production as an ongoing operational responsibility, not a one-time setup task. We’ll connect tools like yum, apt and dnf directly to Ansible, CI/CD pipelines and Kubernetes nodes so you actually understand what you’re automating.

What you’ll learn:
00:00 - Why DevOps engineers must understand package managers
01:01 - Package Management in Real Cloud & Enterprise Environments
01:19 - How DevOps Engineers Actually Use Package Managers (Reality Check)
01:29 - What You’ll Learn in This Video (Scope & Expectations)
01:59 - What Is a Package Manager? (Concept, Not Commands)
02:47 - Where Yum Gets Packages From & What It Really Does
03:15 - RHEL Subscription Manager Explained (Enterprise Reality)
05:33 - When You Don’t Use Subscription Manager (Air-gapped & Internal Repos)
05:52 - Yum Repository Configuration Explained (/etc/yum.repos.d)
07:32 - Core Yum Commands DevOps Engineers Use Daily
11:15 - Production Updates: What NOT to Do & How Updates Really Work
12:14 - Why Blind yum update -y Breaks Production
12:52 - Package Managers Across Distros (Yum vs Apt vs Zypper)
13:37 - Final Takeaway: Why This Layer Matters for Kubernetes & Automation

By the end, you’ll be able to:
- Understand what yum/apt/dnf are doing under your automation tools
- Avoid “works on my machine” package issues in CI/CD
- Debug broken Kubernetes nodes at the Linux layer
- Treat package management as a continuous DevOps responsibility

This video is part of my “Linux for DevOps: From Zero to Hired” series, designed for:
- DevOps engineers
- SREs and Platform engineers
- Developers moving into Kubernetes and cloud-native roles

Next step after package management: learn Linux networking basics, so Kubernetes networking finally makes sense.

videoTitle: Is DevOps Dead? The Truth in 26 Seconds #shorts
videoDescription:
People say DevOps is dying because of AI.

Not really.

DevOps was never just a role. It’s how teams build, deploy, and run software together.

The tools are changing, AI is entering the workflow, but DevOps itself isn’t disappearing.

If you work in cloud or engineering, this shift matters.

#devops #devopsengineer #ai #cloudcomputing #kubernetes #platformengineering #softwareengineering #automation #programming #tech
channelDescription:
Most infrastructure advice works in controlled demos. It often fails under real constraints.

SystemAxis explores how AI, cloud, and DevOps systems behave in production — under load, under pressure, and under real cost limits.

This channel focuses on practical engineering: stress testing configurations, analyzing bottlenecks, understanding failure modes, and making deliberate architectural decisions. Sometimes that means running controlled experiments. Sometimes it means breaking assumptions. Sometimes it means designing systems properly from the start.

Topics include AI infrastructure, cloud architecture, Linux internals, automation, cost control, platform engineering, and performance tuning.

The goal is simple: understand how systems actually behave - not how they are supposed to behave.

No hype. No demo-only advice. Just production reality.

videoTitle: Linux Networking Tools for DevOps: curl, wget, dig & traceroute Explained with Real Demos
videoDescription:
Linux Networking Tools for DevOps: curl, wget, dig & traceroute Explained with Real Demos

In the last video, we looked at how Linux networking works internally.
In this video, we’re going fully practical: the exact tools DevOps engineers use during real production incidents.

You won’t just “learn commands,” you’ll learn what question each tool answers:
• curl – is the service actually responding?
• wget – can I download content / check connectivity quickly?
• dig – does this DNS name resolve to an IP address?
• traceroute – where does the network path break?

By the end, you’ll have a simple 4-step flow you can run every time something is “just not working” in production.

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps  ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Why Debugging Matters More Than Networking Theory
00:37 - curl: Is the Service Actually Responding?
02:34 - wget: Download & Connectivity Check
03:24 - curl vs wget: Which One Should You Use?
03:50 - dig: Does DNS Resolve or Not?
04:34 - traceroute: Where Does Traffic Stop?
05:53 - Real Debugging Flow + What’s Next

What you’ll learn:
• How to use curl to validate services, ports, and status codes
• When wget is better than curl in CI/CD and scripts
• How dig helps you separate DNS problems from app problems
• How traceroute reveals where packets stop on the network path
• A practical, repeatable debug order for real incidents

videoTitle: Linux Firewall Explained for DevOps | firewalld, iptables & Troubleshooting
videoDescription:
Linux Firewall Explained for DevOps | firewalld, iptables & Troubleshooting

Firewall issues are one of the most common causes of production outages in DevOps. Your service is running, DNS works, but traffic still fails. In many cases, the Linux firewall is the root cause.

In this video, you will learn how Linux firewall works using firewalld and iptables, and how packet filtering happens inside the Linux kernel.

This tutorial focuses on real DevOps troubleshooting using practical commands.

Topics covered:

• How Linux firewall packet filtering works
• firewalld explained with real examples
• How to check firewall status and zones
• How to allow ports using firewall-cmd
• iptables explained for DevOps engineers
• INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD chains explained
• How Kubernetes uses iptables internally
• DevOps firewall troubleshooting workflow

This video is part of the Linux for DevOps series.

If you want to become strong in Docker, Kubernetes, and DevOps, understanding Linux firewall is essential.

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬ Timestamps  ▬▬▬▬▬▬▬
00:00 - Introduction: Why Firewall Issues Break DevOps Systems
00:33 - How Linux Firewall Packet Filtering Works (Basic Concept)
01:16 - firewalld Tutorial: Check Status, Zones, and Allow Ports
03:00 - iptables Explained: How Linux Actually Filters Packets
03:44 - INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD Chains Explained (Visual Guide)
04:18 - DevOps Firewall Troubleshooting Mindset + Outro

videoTitle: Introduction to Secure DevOps | AZ-400 | Episode 22
videoDescription:
Episode 22 of 25
For the full video series, click here: https://aka.ms/AZ-400onYouTube

In this episode, you’ll explore DevSecOps and learn how modern DevOps practices integrate security and compliance into every stage of the software development lifecycle. The session examines why security is a shared responsibility, reviews common threats such as SQL injection, and explains how to build secure pipelines with continuous validation.

You’ll also learn how tools such as CodeQL, threat modeling, and automated scanning help identify vulnerabilities early in the development process and reduce risk. The episode concludes with an overview of open-source software, associated risks, and strategies for managing licensing and compliance. videoTitle: Introduction to Secure DevOps | AZ-400 | Episode 22
videoDescription:
Episode 22 of 25
For the full video series, click here: https://aka.ms/AZ-400onYouTube

In this episode, you’ll explore DevSecOps and learn how modern DevOps practices integrate security and compliance into every stage of the software development lifecycle. The session examines why security is a shared responsibility, reviews common threats such as SQL injection, and explains how to build secure pipelines with continuous validation.

You’ll also learn how tools such as CodeQL, threat modeling, and automated scanning help identify vulnerabilities early in the development process and reduce risk. The episode concludes with an overview of open-source software, associated risks, and strategies for managing licensing and compliance. This content aligns with AZ-400 certification objectives related to secure development and compliance practices.

videoTitle: DevOps vs SRE Explained | What's the difference?
videoDescription:
In this video, I break down the key differences between DevOps and Site Reliability Engineering (SRE), two essential approaches to modern infrastructure management. While both aim to improve software delivery and system reliability, their goals, methods, and roles are distinct.

Key Takeaways:
- DevOps: Focuses on collaboration between development and operations teams to enhance speed, automation, and deployment efficiency.
- SRE: Prioritizes system reliability, performance, and accountability through software engineering principles and metrics like SLOs and error budgets.
- Real-World Applications: How these concepts are applied in various organizations, and how DevOps and SRE can complement each other.

#itcareer #realtech #technology #devops #devopslife #devopsculture #devopsengineer #sre #sitereliabilityengineering

videoTitle: AWS Cloud Practitioner vs AWS CloudOps Engineer vs AWS DevOps Professional: Same Problem 3 Solutions
videoDescription:
🚀 AWS Cloud Practitioner vs CloudOps Engineer vs DevOps Professional - Which Certification Path is Right for You?

Discover how three AWS-certified professionals handle the SAME performance crisis completely differently! This real-world scenario breakdown shows you exactly what Cloud Practitioner, CloudOps Engineer (formerly SysOps Administrator), and DevOps Professional certifications mean in practice.

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS:
00:00 - Crisis Scenario Introduction
01:00 - AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner Overview
02:00 - AWS Certified CloudOps Engineer Associate
03:00 - AWS Certified DevOps Engineer Professional
04:00 - Key Differences: WHAT vs HOW vs WHY
05:00 - Real-World Scenario Setup
06:00 - Cloud Practitioner Response (15 min solution)
08:00 - CloudOps Engineer Response (2-3 hour solution)
11:00 - DevOps Professional Response (Multi-phase transformation)
15:00 - Which AWS Certification is Right for You?

🎯 WHAT YOU'LL LEARN:
✅ Complete breakdown of AWS Cloud Practitioner certification and career path
✅ AWS CloudOps Engineer Associate exam details and job responsibilities
✅ AWS DevOps Professional certification requirements and strategic thinking
✅ Real-world troubleshooting approaches for each certification level
✅ AWS Auto Scaling, CloudWatch, and performance optimization techniques
✅ Career progression from foundational to professional AWS certifications
✅ Salary expectations for each AWS certification level
✅ How to choose between Cloud Practitioner, CloudOps, and DevOps paths
💼 CERTIFICATION DETAILS:

Cloud Practitioner: Foundational | 90 min | 65 questions | No hands-on required
CloudOps Engineer: Associate | 130 min | 65 questions + labs | Hands-on scenarios
DevOps Professional: Professional | 180 min | 75 questions | Advanced architecture

💰 SALARY RANGES:

Cloud Practitioner: $50K-$70K
CloudOps Engineer: $70K-$110K
DevOps Professional: $110K-$160K+

🔗 HELPFUL RESOURCES:

AWS Certification Official Page: https://aws.amazon.com/certification/
AWS Training and Certification: https://aws.amazon.com/training/

→ IT professionals considering AWS certification

videoTitle: Space Domain Awareness: DevOps and Software Integration Shaping the Future of Military Operations
videoDescription:
In this panel segment at the AFA Warfare Symposium, Major General Gregory J. Gagnon invites Bryon Kroger, Rise8 CEO and former Kessel Run co-founder to share his invaluable insights on Space Domain Awareness. Discover how DevOps and software integration are shaping the future of military operations, the role of the Unified Data Library (UDL), and the pivotal changes needed to enhance the Department of Defense's IT infrastructure.

🔍 Key Highlights & Timestamps:
[0:00] Introduction by Maj. Gen. Gregory J. Gagnon
[0:57] Bryon Kroger on the pivotal role of software in military operations and challenges in the Department of Defense
[1:36] The impact of enterprise IT problems on software delivery and integration with UDL
[2:16] Kessel Run's contribution to the warfighter and the significance of continuous delivery
[3:00] The importance of learning from deployments and the current limitations of IT infrastructure in the defense sector
[3:52] Insights into the acquisition process, continuous delivery, and the need for feedback from warfighters
[5:27] Challenges of enterprise IT solutions in production and the negative impact of mandated solutions
[5:59] The importance of setting benchmarks for DevOps and focusing on efficacy in IT infrastructure
[6:30] Maj. Gen. Gagnon's reflections on infrastructure issues and the successes with the UDL

videoTitle: Day 23 – Docker with Azure Pipelines | Azure DevOps 30 Days Challenge
videoDescription:
Welcome to Day 23 of my 30 Days Azure DevOps Challenge 🚀

Today’s focus: Docker with Azure Pipelines

🔹 What is Docker?
Think of Docker as a box that contains everything your app needs – code, dependencies, and environment – so it runs the same anywhere.

🔹 What is Azure Pipelines?
Azure Pipelines is the delivery system that builds that box, tests it, and ships it to where it needs to run.

Together, Docker + Azure Pipelines solve the famous “it works on my machine” problem and make deployments fast, reliable, and consistent across environments.

👉 Key takeaway:

Docker = the box for your app 🐳

Azure Pipelines = the delivery system for that box 🚚

📌 Next in Day 24 → we’ll connect Docker with Kubernetes to see how containers run at scale.

✅ Watch the full 30 Days of Azure DevOps Playlist here: [paste playlist link]

#AzureDevOps #Docker #AzurePipelines #Containers #DevOps #30DaysChallenge

videoTitle: Unified DevOps Resilience: Jira Backup with Commvault | Demo
videoDescription:
Jira is a foundational system of record for agile development, IT service management, and operational workflows. In this demo, see how Commvault Cloud Backup and Recovery for DevOps delivers enterprise-grade protection for Jira—helping teams safeguard work items, spaces, and configurations against accidental deletion, misconfiguration, outages, and cyber threats.

What This Demo Covers
* Why Jira data protection is critical for operational continuity
* Express Onboarding using secure Jira OAuth integration
* Defining backup policies:
* Frequency and schedule
* Retention (including long-term or indefinite retention)
* Storage region for data residency
* Automatic and on-demand backups (full, incremental, synthetic)
* Recovery Calendar for point-in-time restores
* Flexible restore options:
* Full Jira site recovery
* Individual space recovery
* In-place or out-of-place restore
* Audit logs and reporting for governance and compliance

Key Benefits
* Reduce risk of Jira data loss or corruption
* Minimize disruption to development and IT workflows
* Support compliance and internal governance requirements
* Enable disaster recovery and migration scenarios
* Protect Jira as part of unified DevOps resilience

TL;DR
This demo shows how Commvault Cloud protects Jira data with automated backups, flexible recovery options, and audit-ready visibility for DevOps teams.

Who Is This For?
* CIOs and IT Leaders
* DevOps and Engineering Teams
* Jira Administrators
* IT Service Management (ITSM) Teams
* Security and Compliance Leaders
* SaaS Backup and Platform Teams

FAQ
Does Commvault support Jira Cloud?
Yes. Commvault Cloud Backup and Recovery supports protection and recovery for Jira environments using secure OAuth integration.

Can I restore individual spaces or work items?
Yes. You can restore entire Jira sites or individual spaces, depending on your recovery needs.

Are backups automated?
Yes. Backups run automatically once configured, with options for on-demand full, incremental, or synthetic backups.

Does Commvault support long-term retention?
Yes. Retention policies can be configured for defined timeframes, including long-term or indefinite retention.

Is audit logging included?
Yes. Backup and recovery activities are captured through detailed audit logs to support compliance and governance requirements.

Get started with a free 30-day trial or request a personalized demo to see how Commvault Cloud protects your Jira environment.

videoDescription:
New 📚 Release! Architecting and Implementing DevOps for Infrastructure Management in Azure: Infrastructure DevOps in Azure: Configure, Deploy, & Automate Your Azure resources with terraform, GitHub Actions and Ansible by Ashish Raj

Embark on a transformative journey from basic to intricate infrastructure configurations—think high availability, scalability, and security—all through the power of Infrastructure as Code. Dive into the automation of Azure resource deployments, leveraging DevOps tools with comprehensive, step-by-step guidance and practical examples. But the learning doesn't stop at deployment; delve deeper into automated governance and monitoring, ensuring your infrastructure is not just built but also managed with precision and efficiency.

#MicrosoftAzure #coding #programming #newreleases #books #bookstan

Link: https://leanpub.com/devopsinazure

About the Book

In the face of accelerated development cycles, Infrastructure teams must align with the culture of rapid deployment and infrastructure versioning. Embracing Infrastructure as Code, Continuous Integration, and Configuration Management practices becomes imperative. Tools like Terraform, GitHub Actions, and Ansible empower infrastructure engineers to implement scalable Infrastructure as Code practices in Azure.

This book serves as a thorough guide for deploying, configuring, and automating Azure infrastructure using DevOps tools. It navigates through cloud architecture patterns with DevOps tools, illustrating their application in Azure solution design. Subsequently, the book delves into the implementation of the architected solution, utilizing DevOps tools like Terraform, GitHub Actions, Packer, Ansible, etc., to address specific architectural requirements with emphasis on scaling the solution architecture and establishing governance, aligning with DevOps principles.

Upon finishing this book, readers will be equipped with the expertise to transform business requirements into Azure-compatible solution architectures, seamlessly incorporating a DevOps approach to deployment. Readers will demonstrate proficiency in executing end-to-end deployment with governance and monitoring proficiently through the consistent application of DevOps tools.

About the Author

Ashish Raj is a technologist and storyteller who helps engineering teams improve their velocity. He believes the biggest challenges facing engineers aren't technical, but not knowing how to tie people, process and technology. With an extensive background spanning innovative startups to leading tech conglomerates, Ashish currently serves as a Cloud & DevOps Architect at Haufe Group.

A fervent advocate and contributor to the DevOps and cloud communities, he shares expertise through webinars and local meetups. videoTitle: DevOps Hands-On | Ansible – Day 1 ( DevOpsFarm Day 43 )
videoDescription:
Welcome to Day 1  of Anisble in the DevOps Full Course by DevOpsFarm! 🚀

This video transcript details a deep-dive tutorial on Ansible, the industry-leading Configuration as Code (CAC) tool.

Here is a complete YouTube package, including the video description, chapters, and tags.

YouTube Video Description
This is your complete, hands-on tutorial for Ansible, the powerful, agentless tool for Configuration as Code (CAC). We will show you how Ansible solves the major DevOps problem of managing and configuring hundreds of servers efficiently and consistently.

We begin by establishing the crucial difference between Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools like Terraform (which creates machines) and Configuration as Code tools like Ansible (which configures those machines).

What you will learn in this video:

➤ Configuration Management Fundamentals: Understand why manual scripting on multiple machines is inefficient and how Ansible automates security hardening, software installation (like PostgreSQL, Grafana), and application deployment.

➤ Ansible Architecture: Learn about the Controller Node and why Ansible is agentless, using standard SSH/SCP protocols to manage hosts, unlike other tools that require an agent installation.

➤ Core Concepts Explained: Deep dive into the four essential elements of Ansible:

➤ Modules: The basic units of work (Python scripts) that perform specific tasks (e.g., ping, shell, mysql).

➤ Playbooks: YAML files that define a set of tasks to run on specific hosts.

➤ Inventory: The list of hosts (IPs, usernames) that Ansible manages.

➤ Idempotence: A key concept ensuring that running the same command multiple times yields the same, predictable outcome, preventing configuration drift.

➤ Terraform vs. Ansible: A clear comparison, including why Terraform is better suited for Cloud (like AWS) and configuration management tools are essential for On-Premise (data center) environments.

videoTitle: AI-Powered DevOps in Cloud App Modernization: Automating Deployments, Monitoring, and Resilience
videoDescription:
Natarajan Ravikumar, Principal Program Manager – Cybersecurity & Analytics at Honeywell International, presents "AI/ML in Healthcare Manufacturing: Building Resilient PPE Supply Chains Through Data-Driven Innovation."

Timestamps:
00:00  Introduction & Problem Context
01:17  Why DevOps Needs AI (Scale, Alerts, Complexity)
03:49  AI-Driven DevOps: Automation to Self-Healing
07:35  Generative AI & Agentic Systems in the DevOps Pipeline
11:17  AI in CI/CD & Deployment Safety (Testing, Canary, Rollbacks)
13:17  Predictive Monitoring & AI Incident Response

The COVID-19 pandemic exposed critical weaknesses in global healthcare supply chains, as hospitals faced severe shortages of N95 masks and other protective equipment. At the same time, most institutions lacked advanced forecasting capabilities, making it difficult to anticipate and respond to rapidly changing demand.

Artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML), when combined with Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES) and Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) platforms, helped transform these challenges into opportunities. AI-enabled inspection systems improved speed and consistency in quality checks, while predictive maintenance models reduced downtime by identifying potential equipment failures before they occurred. Digital worker assistance systems supported faster training, greater production flexibility, and more agile responses to disruptions.

Case studies highlight this transformation in action: one global manufacturer scaled respirator production at unprecedented speed, while an industrial conglomerate rapidly established new PPE lines. These digital-first strategies improved efficiency, quality, and sustainability, even under pressure.

Looking ahead, adoption of these digital capabilities remains uneven, particularly among smaller manufacturers facing infrastructure, regulatory, and talent barriers. However, the lessons from this transformation show that AI/ML is not just a short-term emergency solution but a foundation for resilient, adaptive, and equitable supply chains.


Official session recording from the Applied AI Summit 2025.

In our 7th Office Hours, we introduced key updates designed to make infrastructure management more transparent, scalable, and collaborative. InnerSourcing with Custom Terraform Modules – We introduced custom Terraform modules built on InnerSourcing principles, enabling teams to collaborate and contribute to a shared infrastructure codebase. By applying open-source methodologies within an organization, DevOps teams gain flexibility, efficiency, and reusability while reducing silos.

View Permissions in User Management – Security and governance are critical in modern DevOps workflows. This feature allows granular access control, ensuring that only the right people have visibility into specific resources, balancing security with operational efficiency.

videoTitle: The Truth About AI Job Replacement: DevOps, Coding & White-Collar Work | Performant Professional
videoDescription:
"If you're not replacing 20% of your staff with AI, you're doing it wrong." That's the battle cry from Silicon Valley right now. But is it true? And what happens to the rest of us?
In this episode, Michael Forrester, Chris McNabb, and Louis Puster dive deep into AI's real impact on the tech job market. We're not here to hype or doom—we're here to talk about what's actually happening on the ground in July 2025.
From Claude Code deleting entire Kubernetes clusters to chatbots that can't connect you to a human, we share our real experiences with AI tools and examine whether the productivity gains are worth the chaos being injected into organizations.
Key discussion points:

77,000 tech positions eliminated in 2025 due to AI automation
Why AI replaced creative jobs first (not engineering jobs)
The probabilistic problem: AI that ignores its own guardrails
Junior developers vs senior developers: who benefits more from AI?
The career ladder crisis: if entry-level jobs disappear, where do seniors come from?
Support chatbots that never let you talk to a human (looking at you, AT&T)
Adam Smith, capitalism, and why "a market without morals consumes itself"
How to adapt: you can't refuse the next evolution

→ How is AI affecting your job right now?
